questions.prompt: In a temperate grassland food chain, autotrophs capture roughly 10,000 kJ/m^2/yr from sunlight. Using the 10% rule of ecological energy transfer, approximately how much energy reaches the secondary consumers (small carnivores) on average?
choices: 100 kJ/m^2/yr
10 kJ/m^2/yr
1 kJ/m^2/yr
questions.correctText: 100 kJ/m^2/yr
questions.explanation: Each trophic step transfers about 10% of available energy: producers (10,000) → primary consumers (1,000) → secondary consumers (100). The bulk of energy is lost as metabolic heat at every link, which is why food chains rarely exceed four to five trophic levels.

questions.prompt: Which step of the nitrogen cycle converts atmospheric N2 gas into a chemically reactive form that producers can incorporate into amino acids and nucleic acids?
choices: Denitrification by anaerobic bacteria in saturated soils
Nitrogen fixation by Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules
Ammonification by decomposers acting on organic matter
Assimilation of nitrates by plant roots
questions.correctText: Nitrogen fixation by Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules
questions.explanation: Rhizobium (and free-living cyanobacteria like Anabaena) carry the nitrogenase enzyme that breaks the N triple bond and yields ammonia. Lightning and the industrial Haber-Bosch process are the other major fixation routes; all other listed steps act on already-fixed nitrogen.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes the phosphorus cycle from the carbon, nitrogen, and water cycles?
choices: Phosphorus moves rapidly through the atmosphere as a gas.
Phosphorus has no significant atmospheric reservoir; it cycles slowly through weathering of rock and sediment.
Phosphorus is not required by living organisms.
Phosphorus is fixed from the atmosphere by bacteria in soil.
questions.correctText: Phosphorus has no significant atmospheric reservoir; it cycles slowly through weathering of rock and sediment.
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is sedimentary, not gaseous. It moves from rock weathering into soils, then into producers and consumers, and eventually returns to ocean sediments — a multi-thousand-year loop. That slow turnover is why mined phosphate fertilizer is treated as a finite resource.

questions.prompt: Net primary productivity (NPP) is best defined as which of the following?
choices: The total solar energy striking an ecosystem each year
Gross primary productivity minus the energy producers use for their own respiration
The energy stored in the bodies of top predators
The total decomposer respiration rate over a year
questions.correctText: Gross primary productivity minus the energy producers use for their own respiration
questions.explanation: NPP = GPP - autotrophic respiration. It is the chemical energy actually available to consumers and to long-term biomass storage. Tropical rainforests and estuaries have the highest terrestrial and aquatic NPP, respectively; open oceans and deserts have the lowest.

questions.prompt: Wetlands provide which combination of ecosystem services that economists most commonly value when arguing against their drainage?
choices: Flood buffering, water filtration, and habitat for migratory waterfowl
Lithium extraction and silica mining
Year-round commercial timber yield
Permanent carbon storage with no methane release
questions.correctText: Flood buffering, water filtration, and habitat for migratory waterfowl
questions.explanation: Wetlands act like sponges and biological kidneys — they slow flood pulses, trap sediment and nutrients, and shelter migrating birds. Standard ecosystem-service valuations (Costanza et al.) place wetland services among the highest per-hectare of any biome.

questions.prompt: According to the theory of island biogeography developed by MacArthur and Wilson, an island's equilibrium species richness rises with which combination?
choices: Smaller area and greater distance from the mainland
Larger area and shorter distance from the mainland
Smaller area and shorter distance from the mainland
Larger area and greater distance from the mainland
questions.correctText: Larger area and shorter distance from the mainland
questions.explanation: Large islands sustain bigger populations (lower extinction); near islands receive more colonists (higher immigration). The same logic applied to habitat 'islands' (forest patches surrounded by farmland) is a core argument for keeping reserves large and connected by corridors.

questions.prompt: Conservation International labels a region a 'biodiversity hotspot' when it meets which two criteria?
choices: It lies within 23.5 degrees of the equator and contains at least one nation's capital city.
It contains at least 1,500 endemic vascular plant species AND has lost at least 70 percent of its primary native vegetation.
It supports more than 100 mammal species and is fully protected by national parks.
It hosts more bird species than any neighboring country and exports tropical hardwoods.
questions.correctText: It contains at least 1,500 endemic vascular plant species AND has lost at least 70 percent of its primary native vegetation.
questions.explanation: Hotspots, as defined by Myers and refined by Conservation International, must be both irreplaceable (high endemism) and threatened (heavy habitat loss). Examples include Madagascar, the Atlantic Forest of Brazil, and the California Floristic Province.

questions.prompt: A coral species survives in seawater between 23 and 29 C, but bleaches when temperatures stay above 30 C for more than a few days. This pattern is best described as:
choices: The species' realized niche being identical to its fundamental niche
A narrow range of ecological tolerance that makes the species vulnerable to ocean warming
A type II survivorship curve
A clear case of K-selection unrelated to temperature
questions.correctText: A narrow range of ecological tolerance that makes the species vulnerable to ocean warming
questions.explanation: Species with narrow tolerance ranges experience sharp stress when a key abiotic factor drifts outside their optimal window. Reef corals, with only a few degrees of thermal buffer between healthy growth and bleaching, are textbook examples of climate-vulnerable specialists.

questions.prompt: A population of 500 deer grows to 540 deer in one year, with no immigration or emigration. What is the population growth rate (r) for that year?
choices: 0.04, or 4 percent per year
0.08, or 8 percent per year
0.40, or 40 percent per year
1.08, or 108 percent per year
questions.correctText: 0.08, or 8 percent per year
questions.explanation: Change in N is 40 individuals; r = delta N / N_initial = 40 / 500 = 0.08, i.e. 8 percent per year. Compare this to the human global growth rate of about 0.9 percent to appreciate how rapidly even a 'modest' wildlife r can compound.

questions.prompt: Humans, elephants, and large whales typically show a Type I survivorship curve. Which statement best describes that curve?
choices: Mortality is roughly constant across all life stages.
Most offspring die very young; the few survivors live long lives.
Most individuals survive to old age, with mortality concentrated late in life.
Mortality rises rapidly during the reproductive years and then stabilizes.
questions.correctText: Most individuals survive to old age, with mortality concentrated late in life.
questions.explanation: Type I species invest heavily in few offspring and have low juvenile mortality. Type II (many songbirds, hydra) shows constant mortality; Type III (fish, oysters, many insects) shows enormous early mortality with a long-lived survivor tail.

questions.prompt: A nation's age-structure pyramid is broad at the base and narrows steadily toward the top. What does this shape most likely predict?
choices: A shrinking population in the next generation
Rapid future population growth as a large cohort of children enters reproductive age
A perfectly stationary population
An aging population dominated by retirees
questions.correctText: Rapid future population growth as a large cohort of children enters reproductive age
questions.explanation: Wide-base pyramids — typical of Stage 2 demographic transition countries like Nigeria or Afghanistan — carry built-in 'population momentum.' Even if fertility drops to replacement level today, the existing cohort of young people guarantees decades of continued growth.

questions.prompt: According to demographic transition theory, which stage is characterized by falling death rates while birth rates remain high, producing the most rapid population growth?
choices: Stage 1: pre-industrial, high birth and death rates
Stage 2: transitional, high birth rates and declining death rates
Stage 3: industrial, declining birth and death rates
Stage 4: post-industrial, low birth and death rates
questions.correctText: Stage 2: transitional, high birth rates and declining death rates
questions.explanation: Stage 2 typically begins when improved sanitation, food supply, and basic medicine cut child mortality before cultural and economic shifts lower fertility. The gap between the two rates drives explosive growth, as seen historically in 19th-century Britain and currently in much of sub-Saharan Africa.

questions.prompt: The 1980 Mount St. Helens eruption, the deep trench off the coast of Chile, and the Andes mountains all occur along which type of plate boundary?
choices: Divergent oceanic boundary
Convergent boundary where an oceanic plate subducts beneath a continental plate
Transform boundary like the San Andreas
A continent-continent collision zone like the Himalayas
questions.correctText: Convergent boundary where an oceanic plate subducts beneath a continental plate
questions.explanation: Subduction of dense oceanic crust beneath a continent produces an offshore trench, a parallel volcanic arc (the Cascades, the Andes), and frequent earthquakes. The water dragged down with the slab lowers the melting point of the overlying mantle, fueling explosive volcanism.

questions.prompt: On a standard soil profile, which horizon is the dark, organic-rich uppermost layer where most plant roots, decomposition, and microbial activity occur?
choices: The O horizon, consisting of fresh and partly decayed leaf litter
The A horizon, the topsoil rich in humus and minerals
The B horizon, the subsoil where clays and iron accumulate
The C horizon, weathered parent material
questions.correctText: The A horizon, the topsoil rich in humus and minerals
questions.explanation: The O horizon sits on top as litter, but the A horizon (topsoil) is the mineral-and-humus zone that anchors most plant life and is the layer eroded first when vegetation is stripped — the central concern of the 1930s Dust Bowl and modern no-till agriculture.

questions.prompt: Which atmospheric layer contains the ozone layer that absorbs most incoming biologically harmful ultraviolet radiation?
choices: The troposphere, where weather occurs
The stratosphere, roughly 10 to 50 km above the surface
The mesosphere, where meteors typically burn up
The thermosphere, home to the aurora
questions.correctText: The stratosphere, roughly 10 to 50 km above the surface
questions.explanation: Stratospheric ozone (good ozone) is concentrated near 20-25 km altitude and shields the surface from UV-B and UV-C. Tropospheric (ground-level) ozone is a separate, harmful pollutant formed by photochemical smog reactions — same molecule, very different consequences.

questions.prompt: During an El Nino event, which pattern is observed across the equatorial Pacific?
choices: Trade winds strengthen and cold upwelling intensifies off Peru.
Trade winds weaken or reverse, warm surface water pools in the eastern Pacific, and Peruvian fisheries collapse.
The Atlantic gulf stream halts and Europe cools sharply.
Monsoon rainfall in India doubles compared to a typical year.
questions.correctText: Trade winds weaken or reverse, warm surface water pools in the eastern Pacific, and Peruvian fisheries collapse.
questions.explanation: Slack or reversed trade winds let the warm western-Pacific pool slosh eastward, shutting down the nutrient-rich upwelling off South America. The 1982-83 and 1997-98 El Ninos crashed the Peruvian anchovy fishery and shifted global rainfall — droughts in Indonesia and Australia, floods on the U.S. West Coast.

questions.prompt: Garrett Hardin's 'tragedy of the commons' is best illustrated by which scenario?
choices: A privately owned tree farm replanted after each harvest
A national park where visitor numbers are tightly capped
An open-access ocean fishery where each fleet maximizes its individual catch even as the stock collapses
A community garden plot assigned to one family at a time
questions.correctText: An open-access ocean fishery where each fleet maximizes its individual catch even as the stock collapses
questions.explanation: Hardin's framework predicts that when a shared resource is open-access and individual benefits accrue privately while costs are spread across all users, every user is incentivized to over-extract. The 1992 collapse of the Northern Cod off Newfoundland is the canonical textbook example.

questions.prompt: Compared with vegetated land, paved urban surfaces increase stormwater runoff primarily because:
choices: Asphalt chemically attracts rainwater
Impervious surfaces prevent infiltration, so a much larger fraction of rainfall flows directly to storm drains and streams
City rain has a lower freezing point than rural rain
Buildings increase atmospheric humidity
questions.correctText: Impervious surfaces prevent infiltration, so a much larger fraction of rainfall flows directly to storm drains and streams
questions.explanation: On a forested hillside, 80-90 percent of a typical storm infiltrates or evapotranspires. On heavily paved land, that figure can drop below 30 percent, producing flashy floods and warm, sediment-laden runoff. Green roofs, bioswales, and pervious pavement are the standard mitigations.

questions.prompt: Mountaintop removal mining, widely used to extract Appalachian coal, is most directly criticized for which combined environmental impact?
choices: It is the only mining technique that releases no carbon dioxide.
It dumps overburden into adjacent valleys, burying headwater streams and degrading downstream water quality.
It requires more underground labor than traditional shaft mining.
It produces no measurable change in regional topography.
questions.correctText: It dumps overburden into adjacent valleys, burying headwater streams and degrading downstream water quality.
questions.explanation: The technique strips entire ridge tops with explosives and bulldozes the rubble into valleys as 'valley fills.' EPA studies in West Virginia and Kentucky link the practice to elevated conductivity, selenium, and heavy metals in downstream waters, plus durable loss of forest and stream habitat.

questions.prompt: Which farming practice is generally considered the most sustainable way to maintain long-term soil fertility on sloped cropland?
choices: Annual deep plowing followed by bare-soil fallow
Contour planting with cover crops and reduced or no-till management
Replacing all crops with a single high-yield monoculture
Irrigating heavily before each storm to prevent runoff
questions.correctText: Contour planting with cover crops and reduced or no-till management
questions.explanation: Plowing along the contour breaks the slope into mini-terraces, cover crops keep roots in the ground year-round, and no-till preserves the soil-microbe network and the topsoil's water-holding capacity. Together they cut sheet erosion dramatically compared with conventional intensive tillage.

questions.prompt: Integrated pest management (IPM) is defined by which characteristic?
choices: Calendar-based application of a single broad-spectrum pesticide at maximum legal dose
A multi-tactic strategy that combines biological, cultural, and mechanical controls, using chemical pesticides only when monitoring shows damage above an economic threshold
Total elimination of all insect species in a field
Use of only genetically modified crops with no other management
questions.correctText: A multi-tactic strategy that combines biological, cultural, and mechanical controls, using chemical pesticides only when monitoring shows damage above an economic threshold
questions.explanation: IPM treats pesticides as the last tool rather than the first. By rotating crops, releasing natural predators, and scouting for thresholds, growers cut chemical costs, slow resistance evolution, and reduce off-target harm to pollinators and aquatic species.

questions.prompt: Among the three major fossil fuels, which releases the LEAST carbon dioxide per unit of energy produced during combustion?
choices: Lignite coal
Bituminous coal
Crude oil
Natural gas (methane)
questions.correctText: Natural gas (methane)
questions.explanation: Methane has a high hydrogen-to-carbon ratio, so much of its combustion energy comes from forming water rather than CO2. This is why utilities often present a coal-to-gas switch as a transition step — though upstream methane leakage can erode much of the climate advantage.

questions.prompt: A radioactive isotope used in a reactor has a half-life of 30 years. After 90 years, what fraction of the original quantity remains?
choices: One-half (0.500)
One-quarter (0.250)
One-eighth (0.125)
Zero — it has decayed completely
questions.correctText: One-eighth (0.125)
questions.explanation: Ninety years equals three half-lives. After each half-life, half of the remaining isotope decays: 1 to 0.5, 0.5 to 0.25, 0.25 to 0.125. Cesium-137 (about 30-year half-life) is the practical reason that exclusion zones around Chernobyl and Fukushima persist for many decades.

questions.prompt: Which statement best compares passive and active solar energy use in a typical residential home?
choices: Both use photovoltaic panels to generate electricity.
Passive solar relies on building design (south-facing windows, thermal mass, overhangs) to capture heat; active solar uses pumps, fans, or PV panels to move or convert solar energy.
Active solar requires no equipment and is therefore cheaper.
Passive solar only works in tropical climates.
questions.correctText: Passive solar relies on building design (south-facing windows, thermal mass, overhangs) to capture heat; active solar uses pumps, fans, or PV panels to move or convert solar energy.
questions.explanation: Passive design (Trombe walls, deep overhangs sized to summer-vs-winter sun angles, dense floors that store daytime heat) has near-zero operating cost and no moving parts. Active systems — solar hot-water loops, PV panels — convert more energy per square foot but require pumps, inverters, and maintenance.

questions.prompt: An incandescent bulb converts about 5 percent of its electrical energy into visible light; a comparable LED converts about 40 percent. Replacing the incandescent with the LED in a fixture used 1,000 hours per year reduces electricity use most directly because:
choices: LEDs emit no infrared radiation at all.
LEDs waste far less energy as heat, so much less electricity is needed to produce the same useful lumens.
LEDs run at a higher voltage than incandescents.
LEDs require additional cooling fans that store energy.
questions.correctText: LEDs waste far less energy as heat, so much less electricity is needed to produce the same useful lumens.
questions.explanation: Energy efficiency is useful output divided by total energy in. Incandescents are essentially space heaters that incidentally emit light; LEDs convert a much larger fraction of input watts to photons in the visible spectrum, which is why lifetime energy savings can exceed 80 percent.

questions.prompt: M. King Hubbert's 'peak' concept predicts that the production curve of a finite, non-renewable resource over time will:
choices: Rise indefinitely as long as demand exists
Follow a roughly bell-shaped curve, rising to a maximum and then declining as the cheapest reserves are exhausted
Stay flat forever once extraction begins
Drop to zero immediately after the first well is drilled
questions.correctText: Follow a roughly bell-shaped curve, rising to a maximum and then declining as the cheapest reserves are exhausted
questions.explanation: Hubbert correctly predicted in 1956 that U.S. conventional oil production would peak around 1970. The peak occurs when the easiest, cheapest reserves are gone, after which marginal costs rise even if total demand stays high. The same logic applies to phosphate, helium, and other finite stocks.

questions.prompt: The U.S. Clean Air Act requires the EPA to set National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for which set of pollutants?
choices: Carbon dioxide and water vapor only
Six 'criteria' pollutants: particulate matter, ground-level ozone, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, carbon monoxide, and lead
All synthetic organic chemicals in commerce
Only indoor air contaminants
questions.correctText: Six 'criteria' pollutants: particulate matter, ground-level ozone, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, carbon monoxide, and lead
questions.explanation: The 1970 Act (amended 1977 and 1990) targets these six because each has well-established public-health effects at common ambient concentrations. CO2 is regulated separately under the Act's endangerment finding rather than as a NAAQS pollutant.

questions.prompt: Why are PM2.5 particles considered more dangerous to human health than larger PM10 particles?
choices: PM2.5 particles are more chemically reactive in soil.
PM2.5 particles bypass the nose and upper-airway defenses and penetrate deep into the alveoli, where they can enter the bloodstream.
PM10 particles are made of heavier metals than PM2.5.
PM10 particles are radioactive while PM2.5 particles are not.
questions.correctText: PM2.5 particles bypass the nose and upper-airway defenses and penetrate deep into the alveoli, where they can enter the bloodstream.
questions.explanation: Fine particles (diameter under 2.5 micrometers) reach the gas-exchange surfaces of the lungs, raising rates of asthma, heart attacks, and stroke. Diesel exhaust, wildfire smoke, and coal-plant emissions are major sources, and epidemiology links long-term PM2.5 exposure to measurable life-expectancy losses.

questions.prompt: Which contrast correctly distinguishes stratospheric ozone from tropospheric ozone?
choices: Both are pollutants at ground level and harmful at high altitude.
Stratospheric ozone shields life from UV radiation; tropospheric ozone is a harmful pollutant formed in photochemical smog from NOx and VOCs reacting in sunlight.
Both are deliberately added to the air by refineries.
Stratospheric ozone is produced by car exhaust; tropospheric ozone is produced by phytoplankton.
questions.correctText: Stratospheric ozone shields life from UV radiation; tropospheric ozone is a harmful pollutant formed in photochemical smog from NOx and VOCs reacting in sunlight.
questions.explanation: Same molecule (O3), opposite implications depending on altitude. 'Good up high, bad nearby' is the standard mnemonic. Ground-level ozone irritates lungs and damages crops; the Los Angeles Basin's chronic summer ozone problem is the textbook example of photochemical smog.

questions.prompt: Which combination of pollutants and processes most directly produces acid deposition that damages forests and freshwater lakes downwind of industrial regions?
choices: Carbon monoxide and helium reacting with sunlight
Sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides from fossil-fuel combustion reacting with atmospheric water to form sulfuric and nitric acids
Chlorofluorocarbons reacting with stratospheric ice clouds
Methane from rice paddies reacting with ozone
questions.correctText: Sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides from fossil-fuel combustion reacting with atmospheric water to form sulfuric and nitric acids
questions.explanation: SO2 from high-sulfur coal and NOx from any high-temperature combustion oxidize and dissolve in cloud water, falling as rain or dry deposition with pH commonly between 4 and 5. The 1990 Clean Air Act's cap-and-trade program for SO2 sharply cut U.S. acid rain over a generation.

questions.prompt: DDT concentrations measured in a Long Island estuary food web were about 0.04 ppm in plankton and roughly 25 ppm in fish-eating ospreys. This pattern is best described as:
choices: Bioaccumulation within a single organism only
Biomagnification, the increase in concentration of a persistent contaminant at each higher trophic level
Denitrification of nitrogen across a salt marsh
Eutrophication driven by phosphorus runoff
questions.correctText: Biomagnification, the increase in concentration of a persistent contaminant at each higher trophic level
questions.explanation: Because DDT is fat-soluble and slow to break down, each predator concentrates the load of all its prey. Rachel Carson highlighted this mechanism in 'Silent Spring' (1962); thinning of bald eagle, brown pelican, and osprey eggshells drove the U.S. DDT ban in 1972.

questions.prompt: Cultural eutrophication of a lake most commonly produces which sequence of effects?
choices: Nutrient enrichment → algal bloom → decomposition of dead algae → drop in dissolved oxygen → fish kill
Pesticide spill → immediate fish kill → recovery within days
Sediment deposition → increased oxygen → bumper fish harvest
Acid deposition → loss of submerged plants → increased oxygen
questions.correctText: Nutrient enrichment → algal bloom → decomposition of dead algae → drop in dissolved oxygen → fish kill
questions.explanation: Excess nitrogen and phosphorus from fertilizer runoff, sewage, and animal waste trigger algal blooms; when those algae die, decomposer respiration consumes the lake's oxygen. The Gulf of Mexico 'dead zone' fed by Mississippi River nutrients is a chronic large-scale example.

questions.prompt: A stream receives an untreated sewage discharge. Biological oxygen demand (BOD) rises sharply downstream of the outfall. The most likely consequence for stream life immediately downstream is:
choices: A rise in dissolved oxygen levels because organic matter is added
A fall in dissolved oxygen as bacterial decomposers consume oxygen while breaking down the waste, stressing or killing sensitive fish such as trout
No change, because BOD does not affect oxygen
An increase in cold-water trout populations
questions.correctText: A fall in dissolved oxygen as bacterial decomposers consume oxygen while breaking down the waste, stressing or killing sensitive fish such as trout
questions.explanation: High BOD means a lot of biologically degradable organic matter that decomposers will oxidize, drawing dissolved oxygen down. Sensitive species disappear in a 'septic' zone near the outfall; tolerant species and DO levels recover gradually downstream as the waste is processed.

questions.prompt: The Love Canal disaster near Niagara Falls in the 1970s, where families and a school sat atop a former chemical-waste landfill, contributed most directly to which federal legislation?
choices: The Clean Air Act of 1970
The Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA, or 'Superfund'), 1980
The Endangered Species Act of 1973
The Marine Mammal Protection Act of 1972
questions.correctText: The Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA, or 'Superfund'), 1980
questions.explanation: Love Canal residents (organized in part by Lois Gibbs) documented elevated rates of miscarriages and birth defects after buried chemicals seeped into basements. Public outrage pushed Congress to create the Superfund program to identify, clean up, and assign liability for hazardous-waste sites.

questions.prompt: The 1984 Bhopal disaster in India, in which a Union Carbide pesticide plant released methyl isocyanate (MIC) gas and killed thousands of nearby residents overnight, is most often cited as a case study of:
choices: Long-term, low-dose chronic indoor air pollution
Acute industrial-accident exposure to a toxic gas affecting a dense surrounding community
Bioaccumulation of mercury in marine food webs
Routine, regulated emissions under a strong permitting regime
questions.correctText: Acute industrial-accident exposure to a toxic gas affecting a dense surrounding community
questions.explanation: Bhopal is the textbook acute toxic-release case: a single rapid event delivering enormous doses to nearby populations. It is frequently contrasted with chronic, low-dose exposures (like lead in drinking water) and is used to justify worst-case planning, buffer zones, and right-to-know laws.

questions.prompt: Which body of evidence is most often cited as supporting the conclusion that current warming is driven primarily by human greenhouse-gas emissions rather than by natural variability alone?
choices: Predictions of future weather one week ahead
Ice-core records of CO2 and temperature, the isotopic 'fingerprint' of fossil carbon in the atmosphere, and a stratosphere that is cooling while the surface warms
Improved consumer satisfaction surveys
The fact that summers feel hot in many cities
questions.correctText: Ice-core records of CO2 and temperature, the isotopic 'fingerprint' of fossil carbon in the atmosphere, and a stratosphere that is cooling while the surface warms
questions.explanation: Multiple independent lines converge: paleoclimate data from Vostok and EPICA ice cores; the depletion of carbon-13 and carbon-14 in atmospheric CO2 (consistent with fossil sources); and the vertical fingerprint of GHG forcing — a warming surface paired with a cooling stratosphere — which solar forcing alone cannot produce.

questions.prompt: Which list ranks three common greenhouse gases roughly in order of decreasing global warming potential (GWP) per molecule over a 100-year horizon?
choices: Carbon dioxide > methane > nitrous oxide
Methane > carbon dioxide > nitrous oxide
Nitrous oxide > methane > carbon dioxide
Water vapor > carbon dioxide > methane
questions.correctText: Nitrous oxide > methane > carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: Per molecule over 100 years, nitrous oxide is roughly 270 times as potent as CO2 and methane is roughly 28 times as potent. CO2 still dominates the total radiative forcing because it is emitted in vastly larger quantities and persists much longer in the atmosphere.

questions.prompt: Ocean acidification primarily harms coral reefs, oysters, and pteropods because:
choices: Warmer water dissolves more calcium carbonate spontaneously.
Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering pH and reducing the carbonate ions these organisms need to build calcium-carbonate shells and skeletons.
Sea-level rise crushes their shells.
Ultraviolet radiation bleaches their shells directly.
questions.correctText: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering pH and reducing the carbonate ions these organisms need to build calcium-carbonate shells and skeletons.
questions.explanation: About a quarter of human CO2 emissions enter the surface ocean, where the carbonate equilibrium pulls carbonate ions out of solution and lowers pH. Pacific Northwest oyster hatcheries reported direct larval mortality in the late 2000s, an early commercial example of this effect.

questions.prompt: The 1987 Montreal Protocol is widely cited as the most successful international environmental treaty because it:
choices: Created a global carbon tax that ended fossil-fuel use
Phased out chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other ozone-depleting substances, leading to the observed gradual recovery of the stratospheric ozone layer
Banned all uses of nuclear power worldwide
Established a worldwide cap on methane from livestock
questions.correctText: Phased out chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other ozone-depleting substances, leading to the observed gradual recovery of the stratospheric ozone layer
questions.explanation: Driven by the discovery of the Antarctic 'ozone hole,' the Protocol bound nearly every country to phase out CFCs and HCFCs. Stratospheric chlorine has since fallen and the ozone hole has shrunk on schedule, demonstrating that treaty-based responses can succeed when alternatives exist.

questions.prompt: Which contrast best captures the key structural difference between the 1997 Kyoto Protocol and the 2015 Paris Agreement?
choices: Kyoto required binding emissions targets for developed countries only; Paris uses voluntary 'nationally determined contributions' from essentially all countries.
Kyoto banned all fossil-fuel use; Paris allowed unrestricted emissions.
Kyoto focused only on ocean acidification; Paris focused only on biodiversity.
Paris required binding cuts only for OPEC members; Kyoto had no signatories.
questions.correctText: Kyoto required binding emissions targets for developed countries only; Paris uses voluntary 'nationally determined contributions' from essentially all countries.
questions.explanation: Kyoto's top-down binding cuts applied to Annex I (developed) countries and excluded major emerging emitters, which limited participation. Paris flipped to a bottom-up architecture: every country pledges its own NDC and ratchets ambition over time, broadening participation at the cost of guaranteed cuts.
